name: Aguascebas Reservoir Loop
name_original: Embalse de Aguascebas - circular trailrunning por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
description: This moderate 5.2-mile (8.4 km) circular trail running route around Embalse de Aguascebas offers 633 feet (193 m) of elevation gain in under
region_intro: Jogging routes around Villacarrillo are situated in the province of Jaén, Spain, offering diverse landscapes for runners. The region is characterized by its proximity to the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park, which features abundant water sources, lush vegetation, and rugged rock formations. Beyond the mountainous terrain, Villacarrillo is surrounded by extensive olive groves and cereal fields, alongside river valleys such as the Valle de La Osera. This varied topography provides a range of running environments, from challenging mountain trails to more gentle paths through agricultural lands. Best jogging routes around Villacarrillo The most popular jogging route is Aguascebas Reservoir Loop, a 5.2 miles (8.4 km) trail that takes about 1 hour to complete. This route offers pleasant running opportunities around the Aguascebas reservoir. Another top favourite among local runners is Santo Tomé y Cerró de las Albahacas — circular, a moderate 5.7 miles (9.1 km) path. This route traverses the Úbeda hills, providing views of olive groves and agricultural fields. Local runners also love the Round trip on the Paseo de Vida path — banks of the Río Guadalquivir, a 3.0 miles (4.8 km) trail leading through riverbanks, often completed in about 35 minutes. Jogging around Villacarrillo is defined by mountain ranges, olive groves, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels. The routes in Villacarrillo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 180 runners have used komoot to explore Villacarrillo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: Are there easy running trails for beginners in Villacarrillo?
A: Yes, Villacarrillo offers some accessible running paths suitable for beginners. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1364657566/round-trip-on-the-paseo-de-vida-path-banks-of-the-rio-guadalquivir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Round trip on the Paseo de Vida path — banks of the Río Guadalquivir&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 3.0-mile (4.8 km) trail that takes about 35 minutes to complete, offering a gentle introduction to the area's riverbank scenery.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect on running routes near Villacarrillo?
A: The running routes around Villacarrillo offer a diverse range of landscapes. You'll find paths winding through extensive olive groves and cereal fields in the Úbeda hills, as well as more challenging trails within the nearby Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. This park features rugged rock formations, lush vegetation, and abundant water sources, including rivers and reservoirs like the Aguascebas.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Villacarrillo area?
A: Yes, several running routes around Villacarrillo are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989320195/aguascebas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguascebas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 5.2 miles (8.4 km) long and offers pleasant views around the water. Another is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1367495309/santo-tome-y-cerro-de-las-albahacas-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Tomé y Cerró de las Albahacas — circular&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 5.7-mile (9.1 km) path through olive groves.

Q: What are some notable natural features or attractions I might see while running?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter impressive waterfalls like the Cascada de La Osera, especially in the Sierra de las Villas, often called the 'Sierra del Agua'. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3168204" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco del Aceite&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5411386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco de la Pringue&lt;/a&gt; are beautiful natural pools, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5391992" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tranco de Beas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers expansive water views. Many routes also provide scenic viewpoints, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896738" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Isla Cabeza de la Viña Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Villacarrillo?
A: The running routes in Villacarrillo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 8 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, from serene riverbanks to challenging mountain paths, and the opportunity to explore the natural beauty of the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park.

Q: Are there any challenging long-distance running options available?
A: For those seeking a significant challenge, Villacarrillo is a gateway to the GR 247 Bosques del Sur Trail, which is Spain's longest circular hiking route. While not exclusively for running, its extensive network of 317 kilometers (and additional variants) through the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park offers immense opportunities for long-distance trail running. A specific challenging route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24525582" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de Aguas Negras loop from Aparcamiento Rio Borosa&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 13.6-mile (21.9 km) trail with significant elevation gain.

Q: Can I find running routes that pass by water features like rivers or reservoirs?
A: Absolutely. The region is known as the 'Sierra del Agua' due to its numerous water sources. You can enjoy runs along the banks of the Río Guadalquivir, as seen in the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1364657566/round-trip-on-the-paseo-de-vida-path-banks-of-the-rio-guadalquivir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Round trip on the Paseo de Vida path&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e989320195/aguascebas-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aguascebas Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; also provides beautiful scenery around a large body of water.

Q: What is the typical difficulty level of running trails in Villacarrillo?
A: The running trails around Villacarrillo cater to various fitness levels. While there is one easy route, the majority of the 13 available routes are classified as moderate (8 routes), offering a good balance of challenge and accessibility. For experienced runners, there are also 4 difficult routes that feature more significant elevation changes and rugged terrain.

Q: Are there any specific trails that offer views of the olive groves?
A: Yes, many routes outside the immediate mountainous park areas will take you through the iconic olive groves. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1367495309/santo-tome-y-cerro-de-las-albahacas-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santo Tomé y Cerró de las Albahacas — circular&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, traverses the Úbeda hills, providing excellent views of the extensive olive groves and agricultural fields that characterize the region.

Q: How many running routes are available in total around Villacarrillo?
A: There are 13 dedicated running routes available around Villacarrillo, offering a variety of distances, durations, and difficulty levels to suit different preferences and fitness levels.

Q: Are there any shelters or huts along the running routes in case of bad weather?
A: Within the broader natural park area, there are some shelters and huts that could offer refuge. Notable examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3286409" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casa Forestal La Parra Refuge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896716" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tinada de Serval free shelter&lt;/a&gt;. It's always advisable to check the specific route details for facilities along your chosen path.
tips:
The Aguascebas Reservoir, also known as the Aguaderondo Reservoir, is located in the municipality of Villacarrillo. This reservoir was built between 1967 and 1969 on the streams of Aguascebas de la Fuente del Tajo and Aguascebas de Chorro Gil.
The natural surroundings of the reservoir are impressive, with a karst landscape that includes waterfalls and a rich flora and fauna. Pine forests and riverside vegetation predominate among the vegetation, while common and rainbow trout can be found in its waters.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog the Embalse de Aguascebas circular trail?
faqs.answer: This trail running route is approximately 8.4 kilometers long. Most people complete it in about 1 hour, though your pace and stops for scenery will influence the exact duration.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied. You'll start on well-maintained forest tracks, then encounter a steep descent, followed by stonier and rockier sections. There are also parts with clayey ground and sharp limestone rocks, and some sections run along riverbeds, occasionally requiring crossings via a ford or concrete blocks. Expect some technical sections with deteriorated gravel, dirt, and stone surfaces, along with continuous ascents and descents.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers breathtaking views. You'll experience the beautiful blue waters of the Aguascebas Reservoir and the impressive Chorrogil Waterfall, which is particularly spectacular after rainfall. The path winds through pine forests, Mediterranean scrub, and gallery forests, with vistas of prominent peaks like La Morra and Navazalto. You might also spot historical elements like old farmhouses and threshing floors.

faqs.question: Is the Embalse de Aguascebas circular trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: For jogging, this trail is rated as moderate. It requires a good level of physical fitness due to its varied terrain and elevation changes, including an initial continuous ascent. While it's considered easier for walking, trail runners should be prepared for a challenging but rewarding experience.

faqs.question: What wildlife or plants can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The area is rich in flora, including various pines, sabinas, and wild roses. Keep an eye out for wildlife such as mountain goats, squirrels, foxes, and vultures, which are often seen in the natural park.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for trail running. After rainfall, the Chorrogil Waterfall is particularly impressive. Summers can be hot, so an early start is recommended if visiting during those months.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail is a circular route, and typically starts near the Aguascebas Reservoir. Parking availability can vary, so it's advisable to check local information or arrive early, especially during peak seasons, to secure a spot near the trailhead.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on trails within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. However, it's crucial to keep your dog on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure a pleasant experience for all trail users. Always follow park regulations.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail in the Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the trails within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park for activities like trail running. However, always check for any updated local regulations or specific access restrictions before your visit, especially for certain protected areas or during specific seasons.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route largely follows the official 'Embalse de Aguascebas' trail for about 76% of its length. It also briefly intersects with sections of the '[CIMA JA02] Collado de la Traviesa (Pocico)', 'La Osera', and 'Sendero de la Osera' trails.

faqs.question: What should I bring for trail running on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the varied and sometimes technical terrain, we recommend wearing appropriate trail running shoes with good grip. Bring enough water, especially on warmer days, as water sources might not be readily available. Sun protection, snacks, and a basic first-aid kit are also advisable. A map or GPS device is helpful for navigation.
